full_response:
Let  

• S _CAM (t)   : the k books that Cam has immediately **before** the t-th reading,  
• S _OPT (t)   : the k books that Opal has at the same moment,  

and set  

 S(t) = S _CAM (t) \ S _OPT (t)               (the books that are only Cam’s).

For every b∈S _CAM (t) let  

 w_t(b) = position of b in Cam’s pile                   (bottom = 1 , … , top = k).

Define the potential

  Φ(t) = Σ_{b∈S(t)} w_t(b).                                     (1)

A.  The potential is bounded  
Because every height is in {1,…,k} and |S(t)| ≤ k,

 0 ≤ Φ(t) ≤ 1+2+⋯+k = k(k+1)/2.                             (2)

At the moment considered in the problem statement Cam and Opal possess the
same k books, so S(0)=∅ and

 Φ(0)=0.                                                     (3)



B.  One–step amortised inequality  

Let C_CAM(t)   (resp. C_OPT(t)) be the cost of the t-th request for Cam
(resp. Opal): 1 for a library trip, 0 for a hit.
We prove that for every request t

  C_CAM(t) ≤ k·C_OPT(t) + Φ(t−1) − Φ(t).                   (4)

Adding (4) over the n requests and telescoping Φ yields

 Σ C_CAM ≤ k Σ C_OPT + Φ(0) − Φ(n)                       (5)

and by (2–3)  

 C_CAM(B) ≤ k C_OPT(B) + k(k+1)/2,                        (6)

so Cam is k-competitive with c = k(k+1)/2.



Proof of (4) – case analysis
───────────────────────────
Let b be the book required at step t.

---------------------------------------------------
1. Cam hit, Opal hit   (b∉S(t−1))
---------------------------------------------------
Cam only slides b to the top; books that are in S(t−1) only move
down, hence Φ cannot increase:

 Φ(t) ≤ Φ(t−1).                      

So

 C_CAM=0 ≤ 0 + Φ(t−1)−Φ(t).               ✓



---------------------------------------------------
2. Cam hit, Opal miss  (b∈S(t−1))
---------------------------------------------------
Moving b (in S) to the top raises its weight from
h≤k to k, but every book that was above b
(in S or not) moves down one level.

Hence  

 Φ(t) − Φ(t−1) ≤ k−h ≤ k−1,     so Φ(t−1)−Φ(t) ≥ −(k−1).

Opal pays 1, Cam pays 0:

 0 ≤ k·1 + Φ(t−1)−Φ(t).                     ✓



---------------------------------------------------
3. Cam miss, Opal hit
---------------------------------------------------
Let x be the book Cam removes from the bottom (w=1) and y=b the
newly fetched book (already possessed by Opal).

• If x∈S(t−1)  its removal lowers Φ by 1.  

• Otherwise x∉S(t−1).  Because Cam lacks b while Opal has it,
 S(t−1) cannot be empty (Cam and Opal caches would then be identical
and Cam would have hit).  Hence some book of S(t−1) lies above x; all
such books move down one step, decreasing Φ by at least 1.

Thus in every sub-case  

 Φ(t−1)−Φ(t) ≥ 1.                               (7)

Cam pays 1, Opal pays 0, so by (7)

 1 ≤ 0 + Φ(t−1)−Φ(t).                         ✓



---------------------------------------------------
4. Cam miss, Opal miss
---------------------------------------------------
Both pay 1.  Whatever the changes in stacks,

 1 ≤ k·1 + Φ(t−1)−Φ(t)                            ✓   
 (because k ≥ 1).

All four cases satisfy (4), completing the proof.



C.  Putting everything together
───────────────────────────────
From (5)–(6):

 C_CAM(B) ≤ k·C_OPT(B) + k(k+1)/2.

Therefore Cam’s “LRU-with-stack” strategy is k-competitive with respect to the
optimal off-line strategy.

\boxed{\;C_{\text{CAM}}(B)\;\le\;k\;C_{\text{OPT}}(B)\;+\;\dfrac{k(k+1)}{2}\;} 

Confidence: 0.34
